narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 66.8% chance that you would not pass. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your academic decile rankings from Years 1 and 3, with your Year 3 position standing out as the top influence pushing toward a failure prediction. Being in the lowest decile for Year 3 grades signaled to the model a notable struggle in maintaining performance, unlike the much higher average deciles around 6 for students who passed the bar. Your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor driving the risk upward, again falling well below the typical mid-range deciles of successful bar takers. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.60 emerged as the third most influential factor, actually working in your favor by pulling the prediction toward success—it's stronger than the 3.24 average among those who passed, showing your potential from prior studies. While your LSAT score of 31 is lower than the 37.27 average for bar passers, and your lower-middle family income bracket is less common among them (where middle and upper-middle incomes dominate), these played smaller roles compared to your deciles. Your choice of full-time study aligns well with the 93% of bar passers who went that route, but it couldn't offset the primary academic signals. Compared to typical accepted students, who boast stronger year-in-school rankings and balanced metrics across the board, your profile highlighted early and persistent grade challenges that outweighed strengths like your solid undergrad record. This isn't a judgment on your effort or background—it's the model weighing patterns from past students to forecast bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking academic advising to improve your decile rankings in future terms, or exploring programs with bar passage support tailored for full-time students like you. With targeted improvements, you could strengthen your profile for other schools or reapply stronger next cycle.
